title: Vertebral Trauma and Spinal Cord Injury
subtitle: Block C — Spinal Neurosurgery: Fracture Classification, SCI Grading, Surgical Stabilisation
guidelines_version: AOSpine Classification System 2013 · ATLS 10th Edition 2018 · ACS-COT Guidelines 2019 · NASCIS III Corticosteroid Data
textbook_refs: Vaccaro AR et al. AOSpine Thoracolumbar Spine Injury Classification System. Spine 2013;38:2028-2037
Fehlings MG et al. Early versus Delayed Decompression for Traumatic Cervical Spinal Cord Injury. PLoS One 2012;7:e32037
Bracken MB. Steroids for acute spinal cord injury. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2012:CD001046 (NASCIS)
Denis F. The three-column spine and its significance in the classification of acute thoracolumbar spinal injuries. Spine 1983;8:817-831
learning_objectives: Apply the AOSpine thoracolumbar and cervical injury classification systems to characterise fracture morphology, ligamentous injury, and neurological status
Use the ASIA Impairment Scale (AIS) to grade spinal cord injury and distinguish complete from incomplete injuries with prognostic significance
Identify the indications for emergency surgical decompression in acute spinal cord injury, applying the 24-hour threshold from AOSpine timing studies
Apply Denis three-column theory to assess vertebral stability and surgical need in thoracolumbar fractures
Describe the evidence (NASCIS II and III) against high-dose methylprednisolone use in acute SCI and current guideline recommendations
Manage odontoid fractures, atlas fractures, and atlanto-axial instability using appropriate conservative or surgical algorithms

sections.title: Epidemiology, Primary and Secondary Injury, and Prehospital Management

blocks.content:
Traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) affects approximately 15-40 persons per million per year in high-income countries, with approximately 250,000-500,000 new cases annually worldwide (WHO 2013 estimate). The demographic profile reflects the mechanism of injury: **young males (16-30 years)** account for the majority of traumatic SCI, primarily from motor vehicle collisions (40-50%), falls (20-30%, increasingly important in elderly populations), and sports/recreational activities (10-15%). Violence (gunshot wounds) accounts for 10-15% in some urban populations. **Elderly patients** are increasingly represented due to demographic ageing — low-energy falls onto a rigid surface in the context of pre-existing cervical spondylosis represent a growing cause of central cord syndrome.

**Primary injury** is the irreversible mechanical damage occurring at the moment of trauma: shearing, contusion, laceration, and haemorrhage within the cord parenchyma. This primary injury cannot be reversed by any current intervention. **Secondary injury** encompasses the cascade of pathophysiological processes initiated by primary injury and evolving over hours to days: ischaemia, oedema, glutamate excitotoxicity, calcium influx, free radical production, inflammatory infiltration, and apoptosis. Secondary injury IS potentially modifiable — the rationale for neuroprotective strategies, haemodynamic optimisation, and surgical decompression within the therapeutic window.

**Prehospital management** focuses on: (1) immobilisation — cervical spine stabilisation with a rigid collar and backboard in any patient with significant trauma mechanism or symptoms; (2) airway management — high cervical injuries (C3-C5) may compromise diaphragmatic function (phrenic nerve C3-4-5) requiring urgent intubation; (3) haemodynamic support — mean arterial pressure (MAP) should be maintained ≥85-90 mmHg during the first 7 days post-injury to optimise spinal cord perfusion pressure (SCPP = MAP - intraspinal pressure). Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) in the acute SCI phase is independently associated with worse neurological outcome. The rigid cervical collar should be maintained until CT of the cervical spine can be performed and injury excluded or stabilised.

blocks.title: Mechanisms of Spinal Cord Injury by Age Group
rows: Low-energy falls, minor trauma on spondylotic cord
Central cord syndrome (most common SCI in elderly)

blocks.title: Haemodynamic Targets in Acute SCI
blocks.content: Maintain MAP ≥85-90 mmHg for the first 7 days after acute SCI (ACS-COT 2019 guidelines). Hypotension (MAP <60 mmHg) causes secondary cord ischaemia and worsens neurological outcome. Vasopressors (noradrenaline first-line, phenylephrine second-line) are preferred over aggressive fluid loading to reach MAP targets without cord oedema. This recommendation is particularly important for high cervical (C1-C4) injuries where neurogenic shock (loss of sympathetic tone causing hypotension + bradycardia) can precipitate haemodynamic deterioration.

sections.title: ASIA Impairment Scale and Spinal Cord Injury Syndromes

blocks.content:
The **ASIA (American Spinal Injury Association) Impairment Scale (AIS)** is the universally accepted classification of SCI severity, updated in 2019 as the International Standards for Neurological Classification of SCI (ISNCSCI). It comprises a systematic neurological examination of 28 key sensory points (light touch and pin prick) and 10 key muscle groups bilaterally, yielding motor and sensory scores and a letter grade:

**AIS A** — Complete: no sensory or motor function preserved in the sacral segments S4-S5. The presence of sacral sparing (any light touch or pin prick sensation at S4-S5, or voluntary anal sphincter contraction) upgrades to AIS B or higher. **AIS B** — Sensory incomplete: sensory but no motor function preserved at S4-S5. **AIS C** — Motor incomplete: motor function preserved below the neurological level of injury (NLI), but >50% of key muscle groups below NLI have muscle grade <3. **AIS D** — Motor incomplete: motor function preserved below NLI, and ≥50% of key muscles below NLI have grade ≥3. **AIS E** — Normal: motor and sensory function normal (may be used after SCI recovery).

The **Zone of Partial Preservation (ZPP)** applies only to AIS A injuries and refers to the most caudal level at which sensory and motor function is partially preserved. Sacral sparing — preserved sensation or voluntary anal contraction at S4-S5 — is the key distinguishing feature between complete and incomplete injuries and is one of the most important prognostic determinants. Any degree of sacral sparing (even minimal perianal sensation) indicates anatomical continuity of long tracts and is associated with significantly better neurological recovery prognosis.

Incompleteness syndromes: **Central cord syndrome** (most common incomplete SCI — elderly, extension mechanism; weakness hands>legs; bladder retention; best prognosis for recovery of ambulation ~75-90%); **Brown-Séquard syndrome** (hemisection — ipsilateral motor + proprioception loss, contralateral pain/temperature loss; best neurological prognosis of all incomplete syndromes, >90% ambulate independently); **Anterior cord syndrome** (anterior spinal artery territory — bilateral motor loss + pain/temperature loss, preserved dorsal column function; worst prognosis of incomplete syndromes, <10-20% motor recovery); **Posterior cord syndrome** (rare — isolated dorsal column loss; motor preserved); **Conus medullaris syndrome** (L1-L2 level — mixed UMN + LMN signs, bladder/bowel/sexual dysfunction); **Cauda equina syndrome** (below L2 — pure LMN, flaccid paralysis, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention).

blocks.title: Incomplete SCI Syndromes — Clinical Features and Prognosis
rows: Arms > legs weakness; hand intrinsics most affected
75-90% ambulate; hand function slower to recover
Bilateral pain/temp lost; dorsal columns INTACT

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Sacral Sparing Distinguishes Complete from Incomplete
blocks.content: The single most important finding in acute SCI assessment: SACRAL SPARING. Any preservation of S4-S5 function (perianal light touch, pin prick, OR voluntary anal sphincter contraction) = incomplete injury (AIS B or higher). Incomplete injury has significantly better prognosis. AIS A (no sacral sparing) = complete injury — 90% remain AIS A at 1 year; however, ~5% convert to AIS B-C. Never pronounce complete SCI prognosis at the acute phase without examining sacral segments.

sections.title: Cervical Spine Injury Classification and Atlas/Odontoid Fractures

blocks.content:
**Subaxial cervical spine injuries (C3-C7)** are classified using the AOSpine Cervical Spine Injury Morphology Classification (2015), which assesses: (1) fracture morphology (A = compression, B = tension band failure, C = translational/rotational); (2) facet joint injury (F1-F4 scale: F1 = non-displaced, F2 = displaced <1 cm, F3 = floating lateral mass, F4 = dislocation); (3) neurological status (N0 = intact, N1 = transient deficit, N2 = radiculopathy, N3 = incomplete SCI, N4 = complete SCI, NX = unexaminable). This morphology-based system guides surgical decision-making more reliably than older systems (Allen-Ferguson, Harris).

Cervical facet dislocation (bilateral or unilateral) is a high-energy injury requiring urgent management. **Bilateral facet dislocation** produces anterior subluxation of >50% of the vertebral body diameter, acute cord compression, and often complete neurological injury. **Emergency closed reduction** (Gardner-Wells tong traction or halo ring traction under fluoroscopic guidance) should be attempted in awake cooperative patients before MRI, using incremental weight increases (5 lb per level, starting at 10-15 lb) up to a maximum of 150 lb or one-third of body weight. Once reduced, definitive anterior (ACDF ± discectomy for pre-existing disc herniation to prevent reduction worsening cord compression) or posterior surgery stabilises the spine.

**Atlas (C1) fractures** most commonly result from axial loading (diving, MVA). The **Jefferson fracture** is a burst fracture of the C1 ring (4-part fracture through anterior and posterior arches bilaterally, with lateral mass displacement). Stability is assessed by the **Rule of Spence**: if the combined lateral mass overhang of C1 over C2 on open-mouth odontoid view exceeds 7 mm (>3.5 mm on either side), the transverse atlantal ligament (TAL) is disrupted and the fracture is **unstable**, requiring surgical fixation. Stable Jefferson fractures (TAL intact) are treated in a rigid cervical collar or halo vest for 8-12 weeks.

**Odontoid (dens) fractures** are classified by Anderson and D'Alonzo: **Type I** — tip avulsion (stable, rigid collar); **Type II** — junction of dens and body (highest non-union risk — operative fixation recommended for: age ≥50 years, displacement >6 mm, angulation >10°, comminuted, posterior displacement); **Type III** — fracture through the C2 body (generally treated in halo or rigid collar, good healing rates 80-90%). Type II odontoid fractures are the most commonly tested in examinations — the high non-union rate with conservative management in elderly patients (up to 35% at 6 months) drives surgical decision-making. Surgical options: **anterior odontoid screw fixation** (requires intact transverse ligament, fracture line perpendicular to screw trajectory, no significant osteoporosis) or **posterior C1-C2 fusion** (Harms technique — universal applicability, higher fusion rates).

rows: Rarely required (associated ligamentous injury may require fusion)
Halo vest — risk non-union 20-35% in elderly
Age ≥50, displacement >6 mm, angulation >10°, comminuted, posterior displacement
Halo vest or rigid collar 12 weeks; 80-90% union
Failure of conservative treatment, significant displacement

blocks.title: SCIWORA — Spinal Cord Injury Without Radiological Abnormality
blocks.content: SCIWORA (Spinal Cord Injury Without Radiological Abnormality) describes clinical SCI with normal CT and radiograph but is increasingly recognised on MRI as cord contusion, haemorrhage, or ligamentous injury. True SCIWORA (MRI also normal) occurs in children with more elastic spines and in elderly patients with transient cord compression during extension through a narrow spondylotic canal. Management: MRI is mandatory even with normal CT; if MRI shows cord signal change, manage as SCI with surgical or orthotic stabilisation.

sections.title: Thoracolumbar Fracture Classification: Denis and AOSpine Systems

blocks.content:
**Denis Three-Column Theory (1983)** divides the vertebra into three longitudinal columns: **Anterior column** = anterior longitudinal ligament (ALL) + anterior half of vertebral body + anterior half of disc; **Middle column** = posterior half of vertebral body + posterior half of disc + posterior longitudinal ligament (PLL); **Posterior column** = posterior osseoligamentous complex (POC) = pedicles, facet joints, laminae, spinous processes, supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, flavum. **Stability** requires integrity of at least two columns. Disruption of two or more columns (especially the middle column) indicates instability requiring surgical fixation. The middle column is the 'key' to stability — isolated anterior column injury (simple compression fracture) is usually stable; disruption of the middle column (burst fracture, with or without posterior column injury) indicates potential instability.

The **AOSpine Thoracolumbar Injury Classification System (AOSpine TLICS, Vaccaro 2013)** is the current standard, based on three domains: (1) **Morphology** — A (compression: A0=fracture process only, A1=wedge, A2=split, A3=incomplete burst, A4=complete burst); B (tension band failure: B1=osseous posterior disruption, B2=posterior ligamentous complex disruption, B3=hyperextension with anterior disruption); C (translational injury — any displacement); (2) **Neurological status** (N0-N4/NX as in cervical); (3) **Modifiers** (M1=posterior ligamentous complex injury uncertain, M2=patient-specific factors). The AOSpine system guides treatment: morphology A0-A2 ± mild neurological = conservative; A3-A4 (burst) ± posterior complex injury = surgery; B2/C injuries = surgery.

**Chance fracture** is a seat-belt flexion-distraction injury, classically involving L1-L3. The fracture line passes through bone only (bony Chance — one-level transosseous), or through bone and ligament (ligamentous Chance — flexion-distraction with posterior ligamentous complex disruption). Bony Chance fractures may be treated in hyperextension bracing (success rate ~85%); ligamentous Chance fractures require posterior instrumented fusion. **Critical association**: hollow viscus injury (duodenum, mesenteric tear) occurs in up to 50% of Chance fractures — abdominal CT is mandatory.

blocks.title: AOSpine Thoracolumbar Morphology — A and B Types
rows: Conservative (brace) if <30° kyphosis, no neurological deficit
Conservative or surgical depending on stability
Conservative if <30° kyphosis, no neuro deficit, posterior intact; surgery if symptomatic or instability
Usually surgical — posterior stabilisation ± anterior reconstruction
Hyperextension bracing if bony; surgery if ligamentous

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Chance Fracture and Associated Visceral Injury
blocks.content: Chance fracture = seat-belt flexion injury at L1-L3. In up to 50% of cases, hollow viscus injury (mesenteric tear, duodenal perforation, small bowel) co-exists — often initially occult on clinical examination. ALWAYS order abdominal CT (ideally CT trauma with IV contrast) alongside the spinal CT in any patient with a Chance fracture. Missing hollow viscus injury is a life-threatening oversight in a spine-focused assessment.

sections.title: Surgical Timing in Acute SCI: The 24-Hour Evidence

blocks.content:
The optimal timing of surgical decompression after acute traumatic SCI has been one of the most important questions in spine surgery over the past two decades. The pathophysiological rationale for early surgery is the secondary injury window — decompression within the therapeutic window limits ongoing ischaemia, oedema, and excitotoxicity. The practical question: how early is 'early'?

The **Surgical Timing in Acute Spinal Cord Injury Study (STASCIS — Fehlings et al., PLoS One 2012)** was a prospective multicentre cohort study (n=313, 6 North American centres) of cervical SCI patients (AIS A-D) comparing early surgery (within 24 hours of injury) versus late surgery (≥24 hours). Results: patients undergoing early surgery had a **2.8× greater odds (OR 2.8, 95% CI 1.1-7.0) of achieving ≥2 AIS grade improvement** at 6 months compared with late surgery, adjusting for baseline AIS grade and age. This was the most robust prospective evidence supporting the 24-hour window, even without randomisation. Importantly, early surgery was NOT associated with increased complication rates compared with late surgery.

Based on STASCIS and multiple systematic reviews (Fehlings et al., Spine 2012 — AOSpine systematic review), the **AOSpine Clinical Practice Guidelines (2017)** recommend: early decompressive surgery within 24 hours of acute cervical SCI is SAFE and is associated with improved neurological outcomes. This is Grade B evidence (cohort studies), not yet Grade A (RCT), as no fully powered RCT of surgical timing in SCI has been completed. For incomplete SCI (AIS B-D), the benefit is most clearly established. For complete SCI (AIS A), the evidence is less definitive — earlier surgery does not consistently change AIS A outcomes but may improve partial recovery.

For **thoracolumbar SCI**, timing evidence is less strong but consensus supports early surgery (within 24-48 hours) when technically feasible, particularly for incomplete injuries. The main contraindications to immediate surgery are: haemodynamic instability, polytrauma requiring life-saving abdominal/thoracic surgery first (damage control orthopaedics principle), and inadequate medical stabilisation (coagulopathy, severe hypoxia).

blocks.title: STASCIS Key Result — 24-Hour Window for Cervical SCI
blocks.content: STASCIS (Fehlings 2012): early cervical SCI decompression <24 hours = OR 2.8 for ≥2 AIS grade improvement at 6 months vs late surgery. This is the landmark trial for surgical timing in SCI. AOSpine 2017 guidelines: early decompression within 24 hours recommended for cervical SCI. Note: this means from INJURY time, not from presentation — if presentation is delayed, immediate surgery on arrival is still appropriate. The benefit does not disappear after 24 hours for incomplete injuries, but the evidence is strongest within the first 24-hour window.

sections.title: High-Dose Methylprednisolone in SCI: NASCIS Evidence and Current Guidelines

blocks.content:
The use of high-dose methylprednisolone (MPSS) in acute SCI was supported by the **NASCIS (National Acute Spinal Cord Injury Study) II and III** trials (Bracken et al., 1990, 1997) and became standard of care internationally through the 1990s. However, re-analysis and subsequent evidence have substantially undermined this practice, and current guidelines actively recommend AGAINST routine MPSS use.

**NASCIS II (1990)**: randomised patients with acute SCI within 8 hours to MPSS 30 mg/kg bolus + 5.4 mg/kg/h infusion over 23 hours (the '24-hour protocol') versus naloxone versus placebo. Prespecified analysis: no significant difference in primary outcomes. **Post-hoc subgroup analysis** of patients treated within 8 hours showed modest neurological improvement in motor and sensory scores at 6 months — this was the basis for the recommendation, despite being a post-hoc, non-prespecified analysis.

**NASCIS III (1997)**: compared 24-hour MPSS versus 48-hour MPSS protocol (for patients presenting 3-8 hours after injury) versus tirilazad mesylate. Post-hoc subgroup analysis suggested the 48-hour protocol superior to 24-hour for patients presenting 3-8 hours post-injury — again, a post-hoc finding.

**Critical problems with NASCIS methodology**: (1) primary outcomes were non-significant — neurological benefit was found only in post-hoc subgroups; (2) clinical significance of motor/sensory score changes was marginal; (3) significant complications from high-dose MPSS: wound infection ×2, pneumonia ×3, upper GI haemorrhage ×2, death ×3 in elderly patients. The 48-hour protocol carries the highest complication rate.

**Current guidelines**: The **Congress of Neurological Surgeons (CNS) Guidelines (2013)** and **AOSpine Guidelines (2017)** both state that MPSS administration for acute SCI is **NOT recommended** as a standard of care (Level I recommendation NOT to use). The CNS 2013 guideline explicitly states: 'The administration of methylprednisolone for the treatment of acute SCI is not recommended. Evidence suggests that high-dose steroids are associated with harmful side effects including death without consistent evidence of clinical benefit.' If a clinician chooses to administer MPSS (institutional/individual discretion), the 24-hour protocol should be used rather than 48-hour, and only within 8 hours of injury.

Post-hoc subgroup: motor/sensory improvement at 6 months if treated <8h
Post-hoc analysis only; prespecified analysis non-significant
RCT, n=499, 24h vs 48h MPSS vs tirilazad, 3-8h

blocks.title: High-Dose Methylprednisolone — DO NOT USE Routinely
blocks.content: CNS Guidelines (2013, Level I evidence): high-dose methylprednisolone is NOT recommended for acute SCI. Evidence for benefit is from post-hoc subgroup analyses only; harm evidence is consistent — infection, GI haemorrhage, sepsis, mortality especially in elderly polytrauma. Administering routine MPSS in 2024 is a protocol violation at most major trauma centres. If asked on an exam what NASCIS showed: primary outcomes non-significant; post-hoc subgroup analysis provided the basis for the former recommendation; current guidelines recommend AGAINST use.

sections.title: Surgical Stabilisation: Principles, Approaches, and Complications

blocks.content:
Surgical goals in vertebral trauma are: (1) **neural decompression** — remove compressive bone fragments or disc material from the canal to relieve cord/root compression; (2) **stabilisation** — restore and maintain spinal alignment and stability to allow early mobilisation and rehabilitation; (3) **alignment restoration** — correct kyphosis or translational deformity to normalise load distribution.

**Posterior approaches** are used for most thoracolumbar fractures: pedicle screw-rod instrumentation above and below the fracture level (typically 2 levels above + 2 levels below for a single-level burst fracture — 'long construct'). For neurologically intact thoracolumbar burst fractures with significant kyphosis (>30°) but intact posterior ligamentous complex, short segment (one above + one below = 4 screws) posterior fixation with or without posterior decompression is feasible with careful case selection. Minimally invasive percutaneous pedicle screw techniques reduce blood loss and paraspinal muscle injury compared with open approaches.

**Anterior approaches** are used when the anterior column requires direct decompression (retropulsed fragment compressing cord anteriorly) or reconstruction (multi-level corpectomy). Thoracotomy for T3-T10, retroperitoneal/thoracoabdominal approach for T10-L5. Anterior corpectomy with expandable cage plus plate achieves direct anterior decompression and reconstruction but requires separate posterior fixation for complete stability ('360° stabilisation') in most cases.

**Combined anterior-posterior (360°) stabilisation** is indicated for: complete burst fracture with both anterior and posterior column disruption; fracture-dislocation (AOSpine Type C); failed posterior reduction with residual canal compromise. In practice, the majority of unstable thoracolumbar fractures are managed with posterior-only instrumentation (with or without ligamentotaxis — the principle of using posterior distraction to reduce retropulsed fragments via attachment to the posterior longitudinal ligament) plus optional transpedicular bone grafting.

**Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) fractures** deserve special mention: the ankylosed spine fractures as a 'long bone' — fractures are typically hyperextension injuries, involve all 3 columns (chalk-stick fracture), and carry a high mortality from missed SCI. A rigid ankylosed spine fracture with even a minor mechanism requires CT scanning. These fractures are highly unstable — they require long-segment posterior fixation (3+ levels above and below) through the rigid bone. The rate of associated SCI is 50-60% and perioperative mortality 10-25% due to age and comorbidity.

blocks.title: Thoracolumbar Fracture Surgical Indications — Quick Reference
